pr_title: Enhanced Pagination Performance for High-Volume Audit Logs
head: performance-enhancement-complete

issues.comment: WHAT: The endpoint fails to verify if `organization_context.member` exists before accessing its properties. WHY: For API token or Sentry App authentication, `organization_context.member` is `None`. Accessing `has_global_access` triggers an `AttributeError`, causing a 500 Internal Server Error and completely breaking audit log access for these clients. HOW: Add a null check (e.g., `organization_context.member is not None and ...`) before checking global access.
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: Passing an integer `cursor.value` directly to a `datetime` filter query causes a Django `ValidationError`.
WHY: `OptimizedCursorPaginator.value_from_cursor` returns the raw cursor integer. During `build_queryset`, this integer is used to filter the audit log's `datetime` field (e.g., `datetime__gte=1612345678000`). Django requires `datetime` objects or formatted strings for `DateTimeField` queries, so this crashes the ORM.
HOW: Parse the integer timestamp back into an aware `datetime` obje
issues.label: bug

issues.comment: WHAT: User-controlled pagination offsets bypass bounds checking when `cursor.is_prev` is True, allowing negative values in array slicing. WHY: Django QuerySets explicitly reject negative indexing with an `AssertionError`. An attacker passing a negative offset (e.g., `?cursor=value:-10:1`) can trigger unhandled exceptions, leading to Denial of Service across all paginated endpoints. HOW: Enforce `max(0, offset)` for all offsets to prevent negative indexing.
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: Calling `math.floor(value)` on a `datetime` object in `get_item_key` causes an unconditional `TypeError`.
WHY: The `OptimizedCursorPaginator` is used for audit logs with `order_by="-datetime"`, meaning `value = getattr(item, self.key)` returns a `datetime` object. Unlike `DateTimePaginator`, it fails to convert this object to a float timestamp before applying `math.floor()`, guaranteeing a 500 error when admins hit this endpoint.
HOW: Convert the datetime object to a float timestamp before
issues.label: bug

issues.comment: WHAT: The paginator explicitly encourages large offset-based navigation (both positive and negative) on high-volume tables instead of relying strictly on cursor values. WHY: Using deep SQL OFFSETs causes O(N) full table scans as the database must read and discard all preceding rows (resource exhaustion), and negative offsets will completely crash the endpoint with a ValueError since Django QuerySets do not support negative slicing. HOW: Revert to strictly bounds-checked cursor pagination (keyset
issues.label: performance

pr_title: Optimize spans buffer insertion with eviction during insert
head: performance-enhancement-complete-v2

issues.comment: WHAT: `organization_context.member.has_global_access` is accessed without verifying if `member` is present. WHY: API access via integration tokens or bot accounts can yield a `None` member, causing an unhandled `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'has_global_access'` that results in 500 crashes. HOW: Guard the attribute access by checking `organization_context.member is not None`.
issues.label: bug

issues.comment: WHAT: `OptimizedCursorPaginator.get_item_key` applies `math.floor()` directly to the model's key attribute, which is a `datetime` object because the endpoint queries with `order_by="-datetime"`. WHY: `math.floor()` does not support `datetime` objects and will throw `TypeError: must be real number, not datetime.datetime`, completely breaking the optimized pagination path for administrators. HOW: Inherit from `DateTimePaginator` instead of `BasePaginator`, or convert the datetime to a float timest
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: `list(queryset[start_offset:stop])` executes a negative slice when `start_offset` is negative, which causes Django to raise an unhandled `ValueError`.
WHY: Django QuerySets explicitly forbid negative indexing, meaning the newly advertised bidirectional pagination will deterministically fail with a 500 Internal Server Error, allowing a DoS via malicious negative cursor offsets.
HOW: Remove the `cursor.offset < 0` branches and strictly enforce `start_offset = max(0, offset)` in all cursor pa
issues.label: performance

issues.comment:
WHAT: `ZUNIONSTORE` relies on the default `SUM` aggregation, which sums the scores (timestamps) of duplicate elements present in both sets.
WHY: If a duplicate span payload exists in both `span_key` and `parent_key` sets, its timestamp score will double, making it permanently the "newest" item and immune to `zpopmin` eviction, skewing chronological boundaries.
HOW: Pass the `AGGREGATE MAX` argument to retain the latest timestamp properly without summing.
issues.label: performance

issues.comment: WHAT: Redis operations `zadd` and `zscan` are executed on keys that were previously initialized as `SET`s by the old version of the code. WHY: This causes `WRONGTYPE` errors on existing keys during deployment, persistently crashing the consumer pipeline and Lua script for active traces until the 1-hour TTL expires. HOW: Change the Redis key prefix for span buffers (e.g., from `span-buf:s:` to `span-buf:z:`) to cleanly partition the new sorted set data from existing sets.
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: `CountDevices` executes a synchronous, uncached database scan (`SELECT COUNT(*)`) over a 30-day window on every cache miss during authentication.
WHY: An attacker sending random `X-Grafana-Device-Id` headers can trigger thousands of these queries per second, easily exhausting the database connection pool and blocking HTTP workers since the count query scales with the table size.
HOW: Maintain the total device count in a high-performance memory store or cache it with a short TTL, rather tha
issues.label: performance

issues.comment:
WHAT: A TOCTOU race condition in `CreateOrUpdateDevice` allows concurrent requests to exceed the configured device limit.
WHY: `CountDevices` and the subsequent `INSERT` operation are executed in separate database transactions without locks; multiple concurrent requests from new devices can all pass the `count < limit` check simultaneously and insert their devices, causing the total active devices to exceed the configured `deviceLimit`.
HOW: Consider using a serializable transaction, adding a ta
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: The authentication logic fails open on database or validation errors, bypassing the device limit.
WHY: An attacker can intentionally trigger a database error during the device insertion/update (e.g., by sending a `User-Agent` or `X-Forwarded-For` header longer than the 255-character database schema limit). `Authenticate` logs the database error but still grants anonymous access, effectively bypassing the quota check.
HOW: If `a.cfg.AnonymousDeviceLimit > 0`, enforce a fail-closed policy by
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: `TagDevice` is now executed synchronously during authentication, blocking the HTTP goroutine on a database operation.
WHY: Previous implementation correctly used a goroutine to avoid blocking the request. Moving this DB call directly into the authentication path ties up HTTP goroutines and increases response latency for all anonymous requests.
HOW: Enforce the limit asynchronously by allowing the request to proceed and invalidating the session later, or offload the limit counting to a fast
issues.label: performance

issues.comment:
WHAT: The device limit enforcement can be completely bypassed by omitting the `X-Grafana-Device-Id` header from requests.
WHY: When the header is missing, `TagDevice` immediately returns `nil` (success). Because `Authenticate` interprets a `nil` error as successful tagging, it grants anonymous access without incrementing the database count or checking the limit.
HOW: If `a.cfg.AnonymousDeviceLimit > 0`, explicitly reject the authentication when the `X-Grafana-Device-Id` header is missing, or enf
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: The local cache bypasses the anonymous device limit on subsequent requests when the limit is reached.
WHY: `tagDeviceUI` caches the `deviceID` before executing the database insert; if `CreateOrUpdateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, the failed device remains in the local cache, causing all subsequent requests from that attacker/device to bypass the DB check and successfully authenticate.
HOW: Move the cache insertion to execute only after the database operation succeeds, or delete t
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: Returning the error bubbles up `ErrDeviceLimitReached` without invalidating the local cache entry that was preemptively created in `tagDeviceUI`.
WHY: Because the rejected device ID remains cached, a subsequent request with the same ID will hit the cache, return without error, and successfully authenticate—completely bypassing the device limit for the next 15 minutes.
HOW: Clear the cache entry if `tagDeviceUI` returns an error, ensuring rejected devices are not cached as valid.
issues.label: performance

issues.comment:
WHAT: The `permDenialCache` uses an unbounded in-memory cache to store user-controlled resource names (`Name` and `ParentFolder`), leading to a memory exhaustion Denial of Service (DoS) vulnerability.
WHY: An authenticated attacker can send continuous requests to nonexistent, randomly generated, long dashboard UIDs (e.g., via `GET /api/dashboards/uid/{random_uid}`). Each unique request adds a new entry to the cache map, which is not bounded by an LRU eviction policy or capacity limit, potentiall
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: The cache fallback in `Check` causes an N+1 database query problem when verifying multiple unpermitted resources, while failing to consistently achieve its goal of instant access. 
WHY: When `checkPermission` returns false on cached permissions, it unconditionally falls back to querying the database. This causes an N+1 query issue when checking multiple distinct unpermitted items. Ironically, if a user checks an item *before* being granted access, `permDenialCache` blocks access for 30 sec
issues.label: bug

pr_title: FEATURE: per-topic unsubscribe option in emails
head: topic-email-management

issues.comment:
WHAT: Missing `nil` check on the `TopicUser` lookup before accessing its notification level.
WHY: If a user is tricked into visiting the unsubscribe link for a topic they have not previously interacted with (so no `TopicUser` record exists in the DB), `tu` will be `nil`. Calling `tu.notification_level` causes a `NoMethodError`, leading to an unhandled 500 Internal Server Error.
HOW: Check if `tu` is present before accessing its attributes.
issues.label: security

issues.comment: WHAT: The `unsubscribe` action modifies the database via an HTTP GET request without any CSRF token verification. WHY: Rails' `protect_from_forgery` inherently ignores GET requests (treating them as idempotent). Because `slugs_do_not_match` redirects blindly, an attacker can embed `` on a malicious site, and any authenticated Discourse user viewing it will be silently unsubscribed from topic 123. HOW: Either require a cryptographical
issues.label: security

pr_title: Async import of the appStore packages
head: appstore-async-improvements

issues.comment: WHAT: Eager execution of dynamic imports in object properties defeats lazy loading. WHY: `import()` executes instantly when the `appStore` object is defined, causing 30 background chunks to load concurrently on application boot, which spikes memory and I/O. HOW: Change the properties to factory functions: `applecalendar: () => import('./applecalendar')`, and update callers to invoke the function: `await appStore[appName]()`.
issues.label: performance

issues.comment:
WHAT: The PR changed a synchronous `forEach` callback to an `async` callback inside a `try/catch` block.
WHY: Errors thrown inside an `async` callback passed to `forEach` become unhandled promise rejections and will completely bypass the surrounding `try/catch` block, silently failing to delete calendar/video events if an error occurs.
HOW: Use `await Promise.all(bookingRefsFiltered.map(async (bookingRef) => { ... }))` so that the returned promises are awaited and any errors are successfully cau
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: `replaceAll` is called directly on `req.body.backupCode` without verifying that it is a string.
WHY: If an attacker submits a malformed JSON payload where `backupCode` is an array or an object, `.replaceAll()` will throw an unhandled `TypeError`, resulting in a 500 Internal Server Error.
HOW: Add a type check to ensure `req.body.backupCode` is a string before invoking string methods on it.
issues.label: bug

issues.comment:
WHAT: The authentication flow allows an attacker to bypass the password requirement entirely by providing a valid `backupCode` alongside a dummy `totpCode`.
WHY: The existing condition `if (user.password && !credentials.totpCode)` skips verifying the user's password if any `totpCode` is present in the request. Because the newly added `backupCode` validation logic executes immediately afterward, an attacker can send a request containing both a valid `backupCode` and an arbitrary `totpCode`, causi
issues.label: security

issues.comment:
WHAT: Calling `idp.isEnabled()` inside the stream filter triggers an N+1 query pattern to fetch organization status.
WHY: The `isEnabled()` method of the wrapped `OrganizationAwareIdentityProviderModel` dynamically calls `provider.getById(getOrganizationId())`, which causes O(N) cache or database lookups on the hot path (login page render) when a realm has many public IDPs.
issues.label: performance
